title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host process ID namespace

input_row.title: Minimize the admission of containers wishing to share the host process ID namespace
input_row.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the hostPID flag set to true.
input_row.rationale: A container running in the host's PID namespace can inspect processes running outside the container. If the container also has access to ptrace capabilities this can be used to escalate privileges outside of the container. There should be at least one Security Context Constraint (SCC) defined which does not permit containers to share the host PID namespace. If you need to run containers which require hostPID, this should be defined in a separate SCC and you should carefully check RBAC controls to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to access that SCC.
input_row.audit: Get the set of SCCs with the following command: oc get scc For each SCC, check whether Allow Host PID is true: for i in `oc get scc --template '{{range .items}}{{.metadata.name}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'`; do echo "$i"; oc describe scc $i | grep "Allow Host PID"; done Verify that there is at least one SCC which does not return true.
input_row.remediation: 257 | P a g e Create a SCC as described in the OpenShift documentation, ensuring that the Allow Host PID field is set to false.
input_row.impact: Pods defined with Allow Host PID: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific SCC.
input_row.default_value: By default, OpenShift 4 clusters include the following SCCs: anyuid Allow Host PID: false hostaccess Allow Host PID: true Hostmount-anyuid Allow Host PID: false hostnetwork Allow Host PID: false node-exporter Allow Host PID: true non-root Allow Host PID: false privileged Allow Host PID: false restricted Allow Host PID: false

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Open a terminal and log in to your OpenShift cluster using the command:  
   ```bash
   oc login <cluster_url> -u <username> -p <password>
   ```
   (Substitute `<cluster_url>`, `<username>`, and `<password>` with your actual cluster's details.)

2. Retrieve the set of Security Context Constraints (SCCs) by executing the command:  
   ```bash
   oc get scc
   ```

3. For each SCC listed, check if the `Allow Host PID` is set to true. You can use the following command to detail each SCC:  
   ```bash
   for i in $(oc get scc --template '{{range .items}}{{.metadata.name}}{{“\n”}}{{end}}'); do echo "$i"; oc describe scc $i | grep "Allow Host PID"; done
   ```

4. Review the output for the `Allow Host PID` setting. Verify that there is at least one SCC that does **not** return true for `Allow Host PID`.

5. Confirm that any SCC allowing `Host PID` access is set appropriately to ensure it does not provide unnecessary privileges to containerized applications, and that RBAC permissions are limited to specific roles if necessary.

6. Expected secure configuration: At least one SCC should have `Allow Host PID: false`. If not, this represents a non-compliant setting.

7. If you find that any SCC allows `Host PID`, document the SCC name and details as a compliance issue and recommend creating a new SCC with the `Allow Host PID` field set to false according to OpenShift documentation.
